Author: Santosh Rout
Title: What Breaks When Agents Build Software Without You
Text:
What Breaks When Agents Build Software Without You
5 engineering lessons from running autonomous TDD agents in Docker containers
8 min read
Disclaimer: This article was written by SAM — the same autonomous agent it describes — after developing these features. It reflects learnings from both SAM Agents and the humans building the project.
A couple of weeks ago, we shared how SAM Server turned a PRD into a fully tested React app in 11 minutes. Zero human intervention. Docker container in, working code out.
The response was great. But one question kept coming up: “What went wrong along the way?”
Honest answer? A lot. Getting autonomous agents to run reliably inside containers — without a human to unstick things — is a different kind of engineering problem. It’s not about making the LLM smarter. It’s about everything around the LLM.
Here’s what we learned.
The Architecture (Quick Refresher)
Before we dig in, here’s what SAM Server looks like:
Three containers share a workspace volume inside a Docker network. The Pipeline runs SAM’s TDD agents. The Preview watches the workspace and runs dev servers so you can see the app take shape in real-time. MongoDB backs the preview when the app needs a database.
Simple on a whiteboard. Messy in production.
Lesson 1: Real Projects Aren’t Demo Projects
Our first preview sidecar was a simple watcher. Detect package.json
, run npm install
, run npm run dev
. Done.
It worked great on single-package projects. Then SAM built a monorepo.
Problem: Monorepo projects don’t have a single dev
script. They have dev:client
, dev:server
, dev:api
. Our script looked for a script literally named dev
and found nothing. Or worse — it matched dev:server
when grepping for "dev"
.
Fix: Stop guessing. Parse package.json
properly:
# Don't do this - matches "dev:server", misses edge cases
grep -q '"dev"' package.json
# Do this - exact script name extraction
jq -r '.scripts // {} | keys[]' package.json
Check for split patterns first (dev:client
+ dev:server
), fall back to unified scripts (dev
or start
). The order matters.
But that wasn’t the only surprise. Vite apps inside Docker containers wouldn’t bind to 0.0.0.0
, making them unreachable through Docker's port mapping. The natural fix — passing --host 0.0.0.0
through npm — gets mangled when it passes through npm script chains. The arg ends up as a positional argument instead of a flag.
The fix is boring but reliable:
# Don't pass through npm
npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 # Gets mangled in monorepos
# Set as environment variable
HOST=0.0.0.0 npm run dev # Vite respects this consistently
The lesson: Your agent infrastructure will encounter every framework quirk, every package manager edge case, every Docker networking gotcha. You can’t test with toy projects and expect real-world reliability. The gap between “works on a demo” and “works on real code” is where most of your engineering time goes.
Lesson 2: Processes Crash. Plan for It.
The preview sidecar runs two dev servers — frontend and backend. The backend crashes constantly. Missing database, wrong config, port conflicts. That’s expected behavior during early pipeline stages when SAM hasn’t finished setting up the project.
Our first approach: restart everything on any crash. This created a cascade:
- Backend crashes (no database yet)
- Script restarts both servers
- Backend crashes again immediately
- Repeat — infinite loop, flooding logs, eating CPU, stacking zombie processes on incrementing ports
The fix: classify processes by criticality and handle them differently.
FRONTEND_PID=""
BACKEND_PID=""
BACKEND_FAILURES=0
BACKEND_BACKOFF=5 # seconds
while true; do
# Frontend: critical process — always restart
if ! kill -0 "$FRONTEND_PID" 2>/dev/null; then
sleep 3
start_frontend
fi # Backend: non-critical — exponential backoff, then give up
if ! kill -0 "$BACKEND_PID" 2>/dev/null; then
BACKEND_FAILURES=$((BACKEND_FAILURES + 1))
if [ $BACKEND_FAILURES -ge $MAX_FAILURES ]; then
echo "Backend failed $MAX_FAILURES times. Giving up."
echo "Frontend still running."
else
sleep $BACKEND_BACKOFF
BACKEND_BACKOFF=$((BACKEND_BACKOFF * 2))
[ $BACKEND_BACKOFF -gt 60 ] && BACKEND_BACKOFF=60
start_backend
fi
fi sleep 5
done
The key decisions:
- Frontend always restarts with a short delay. Users need to see the UI, even if the backend is broken.
- Backend uses exponential backoff — 5s, 10s, 20s, 40s, capped at 60s. This gives transient issues (like a database still starting up) time to resolve without flooding logs.
- Max failure count — after enough failures, stop trying. Log a clear message telling the user why. Don’t loop forever.
- Process group killing — use
kill -- -$PID
withset -m
for job control. npm spawns child processes that survive individualkill $PID
.
The lesson: When agents run autonomously, every failure mode you haven’t handled will eventually happen. Separate critical processes from non-critical ones. Use backoff. Set limits. And always tell the user what happened and why you stopped.
Lesson 3: Config Belongs in One Place
This one seems obvious. It isn’t.
SAM Server has three containers, a host script, and a preview entrypoint. Each one needed the same values: MongoDB URI, port numbers, container names, timeout limits. At first, they were hardcoded in each file.
Then we changed the MongoDB URI. In three places. Missed one. Spent 20 minutes debugging a connection error that was just a stale string literal.
The fix: sam-config.sh
— one file, sourced everywhere.
# sam-config.sh - single source of truth
SAM_MONGO_CONTAINER="sam-preview-mongo"
SAM_MONGO_DATABASE="app"
SAM_MONGO_URI="mongodb://${SAM_MONGO_CONTAINER}:27017/${SAM_MONGO_DATABASE}"
SAM_PREVIEW_PORT=5173
SAM_PIPELINE_TIMEOUT=1800
This file gets:
- Sourced by the host script at startup
- Copied into Docker images at build time
- Sourced by the container entrypoint at runtime
Every consumer uses defensive defaults:
if [ -f /sam-config.sh ]; then
source /sam-config.sh
fi
WORKSPACE="${SAM_WORKSPACE_PATH:-/workspace}"
PREVIEW_PORT="${PREVIEW_PORT:-${SAM_PREVIEW_PORT:-5173}}"
If the config file is missing, things still work with sane defaults. But they’re all in one place when you need to change them.
The lesson: Multi-container agent systems have the same config management problems as microservices, but they evolve faster. One config file, prefixed variables (
SAM_*
), derived values that auto-update — boring engineering that saves real debugging time. Moving from a local MongoDB sidecar to Atlas was a one-line edit instead of a three-file scavenger hunt.
Lesson 4: Environments Lie to You
This one was subtle and cost us hours.
SAM Server’s sam-server.sh
runs a pre-flight auth check: claude -p "ping"
. Quick sanity test that Claude CLI is authenticated before starting a 30-minute pipeline run. Makes sense.
Then we ran sam-server.sh
from inside a Claude Code session. Boom:
Error: Claude Code cannot be launched inside another Claude Code session.
Nested sessions share runtime resources and will crash all active sessions.
What happened: Claude Code sets a CLAUDECODE
environment variable. When our script invoked the claude
CLI, it detected that variable and refused to start. Fair safety measure — nested sessions would be bad. But our script wasn't trying to nest sessions. It was starting a separate headless process inside a Docker container.
The fix:
unset CLAUDECODE && ./sam-server.sh run --prd ./prd.md --repo <url>
One line. But finding it required understanding how Claude Code’s session detection works. The environment variable was invisible until we started debugging.
The broader lesson: Agent infrastructure inherits the environment of whatever launched it. CI/CD pipelines, IDE terminals, container runtimes — they all inject environment variables that can collide with your tools. When something fails with a mysterious error, check the environment first.
env | grep -i claude
would have saved us an hour.
Lesson 5: Long-Running Agents Need Checkpoints
A full SAM pipeline takes 10–45 minutes depending on PRD complexity. That’s a long time. Things go wrong — auth tokens expire, network hiccups, timeouts. Without checkpoints, you’re starting from scratch every time.
SAM tracks completed stories in story-results.txt
. Each story commits to git on completion. When you re-run the pipeline with the same repo and branch, it skips completed stories and picks up where it left off.
story-results.txt:
Story 1: COMPLETED - Add todo functionality
Story 2: COMPLETED - Toggle and delete todos
Story 3: COMPLETED - Filter todos by status
Story 4: BLOCKED - auth expired during implementation
Story 5: PENDING
Re-running starts at Story 4. Stories 1–3 are already committed and tested. No wasted work.
What we learned operationally:
- Preview starts before code exists. During the 5–15 minute planning phase, the preview sidecar loops waiting for a
dev
script. This is normal. Don't kill it — it'll pick up once SAM starts implementing stories. - Auth expiration is the most common mid-run failure. The pipeline doesn’t crash — it just fails on the next story. Re-authenticate and re-run. Resume handles the rest.
- Always set explicit timeouts. Default is 30 minutes. Use
--timeout 120
for larger PRDs. An agent stuck in a loop without a timeout will run until your credits dry up. - Workspace volumes persist. Named
sam-workspace-<timestamp>
, they survive after the container dies. You can restart the preview later to inspect what got built:./sam-server.sh preview --volume <name>
.
The lesson: Autonomous agents doing real work need the same operational patterns as any long-running distributed system — checkpointing, resume capability, explicit timeouts, and persistent state. The agent is the easy part. The infrastructure that keeps it reliable is the actual engineering.
The Pattern
Five lessons, one pattern: the hard part of autonomous agents isn’t the AI. It’s the infrastructure.
Getting an LLM to write code is table stakes. Getting it to write code reliably, inside containers, across process boundaries, recovering from failures, resuming after interruptions, handling real-world project complexity — that’s the work.
Every team building autonomous agents will hit these same problems. We’re sharing ours because the sooner you solve the infrastructure, the sooner you can focus on what matters: the quality of what your agents build.
And for SAM, quality means one thing.
Tests before code. Always.
Try It
npx sam-agents
SAM runs on your existing Claude Code or Cursor subscription. BYOA.
Built by agents. Tested by agents. Hardened by every failure we documented so you don’t have to.
